item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really enjoyed this one. I love stories about mountaineering and adding in the space travel element makes it even creepier.

I'm curious why the SCP doesn't manifest during clear weather though. Is it some sort of extra-dimensional rift? Either way, definitely still a quality entry and gets a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one, particularly the reveal that there really isn't a "monster" in the expected sense.

Also, that it takes advantage of a rather underappreciated suspense trope: Space suits where you don't expect there to be a space suits are creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very fascinating and fun to read description but honestly it was the exploration logs that really did it for me! Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is beautifully creepy. I love how it combines sort of that Cold War/Space Race paranoia with this kind of Area 51 vibe. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh, You're right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wrong number, mate. I think you meant SCP-1529

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted but this one reminds me SCP-1592 and SCP-1959.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This image is borrowed from: Wikipedia

Original Source: flickr
Creator: Maria Ly

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like this. I mean that in a good way. This is freaking me the hell out and I have no clue why. Like, I'm literally cringing from how unsettling this is.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My initial thought was "it's 1959 meets 1529" (thanks whoever posted those!), but then I decided to give one of the exploration logs a shot, and boy was I not sorry. The backstory for this is just perfect, the right amount of information and the right amount of "what the fuck" to be thoroughly tantalizing and a little chilling. Maybe it's just the multi-page format or the callback to those two skips, but this really feels like something from a lot earlier in the site, and it's great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find the last report to be rather unnecessary, but it didn't kill the fun of the SCP. Easy +1 cause I love well done logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's clearly heavily reminiscent of the King of the Mountain along with SCP-1959, but I found myself enjoying it nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't help it, I love good exploration logs. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just about what's going on, yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not related to those two, but I do like both of those considerably, and I won't deny that I'm borrowing from them conceptually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this one a lot. I liked how it just kind of kept jack-knifing back and forth, with little predictability. Generally, I really dislike separate addenda pages these days, but these were used rather well.

If I have any complaint…

+ show block
– hide block

It's that I found the punchline in the Black Eagle log cheesy. Looking back at it in the context of the visual distortion on the -1s, it makes sense, but it's still… like…

Bear with me, I'm having trouble articulating this. The article spends a lot of time being very tense and serious due to the deadly-mountain-climbing subject matter, then the tone very much flips to "oddball/wtf" in Log C, especially where, after getting real weird, the scip basically saves the remaining team members. So far so good, enjoying all of this.

The Orpheus log flips tone again, ending up on the amped-up-superpowered-conspiracy-theory style I've only really seen in Warren Ellis comics. (Giving the astronaut a name was an excellent touch, by the way. It really tied down the rest of the Black Eagle stuff to something "real", and distinguished the person being interviewed from the Foundation.) Anyway, about halfway through this log, I've sufficiently switched gears enough to buy into this.

Then the phone call comes in, and the buildup is intense… but the contents of the phone call have flipped tone again. Now we're back to wtf territory, only now it seems pretty arcane and somewhat mystical/magical. I wasn't ready for a hard right turn like that, and compared to log C it left me kind of unsatisfied. I can't tell if I'm sad that it didn't answer enough of my questions or if it answered too many of them (by inserting -possibly- some kind of escaped world-ending super-weapon behind everything else that was going on).


But, despite my writing four paragraphs about it, it's not meant to be a huge complaint. Definitely +1 in any case!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well we've got Everest and K2, next we just need a homicidal entity haunting Kangchenjunga.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Weird idea, but otherwise interesting. I love the exploration logs and it is frightening.
Well done, keep continue writing SCPs Doctor Anborough, they're very fascinating.
Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This wouldn't happen to interfere or be related to these two, right?

http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-1959
http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-1529

Just saying. You're article is fine, but it feels too similar to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the mystery and overall atmosphere of this, but I'm not quite sure I get it. Is it something like

+ show block
– hide block

Black Eagle develops weapon to eradicate Soviets that could also annihilate the world, astronaut crashes shuttle into mountain to save world, weapon causes anomaly in mountain, and astronaut kills people using the anomaly so they can become the new guardians of the shuttle?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
omg not a keter burn the witch
This is really good, and like Procyon said, I was not at all expecting what happened to happen. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The exploration logs and Orpheus document all have XXXXs where I assume there should be 2585s. Ignoring that, I liked it, it's just mysterious enough, and you gave it an interesting angle. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, certainly wasn't expecting that to go where it did. Thoroughly well done.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… just the bare bones of a name, all rock and ice and storm and abyss. It makes no attempt to sound human. It is atoms and stars. It has the nakedness of the world before the first man – or of the cindered planet after the last.

-Fosco Maraini
